caption.text:
A town of millionaires. A fort built with stolen money. An earthquake that moved a river 150 kilometres away.

Lakhpat, in the Kutch district of Gujarat, was founded by Rao Lakhpatji in the mid-1700s and fortified in 1801 by Jamadar Fateh Muhammad. It was a major trading port on the Indus River, with over 10,000 inhabitants living in luxury within 7 kilometres of fort walls. The name itself comes from Lakhpati — millionaire.

The documented fact: on the evening of 16 June 1819, an earthquake estimated at magnitude 7.7 to 8.2 struck the Rann of Kutch. The Indus River changed course by 150 kilometres. Lakhpat lost its water, its port, and its livelihood. The population dropped from over 10,000 to a few hundred struggling to survive.

But here is the gap. The fort was built with funds looted from the neighbouring town of Pir, whose king died of grief while on pilgrimage to Hajj. Local legend says he cursed Lakhpat before he died. The fort lasted 18 years. Then the earthquake came.

A river does not move 150 km because of a curse. But a town that loses its river, its trade, and its people in a single generation does not recover either. Was it geology — or something older?

text.line_2: Then the river moved 150 km away.
with looted gold from a dying king.
